elements.sent-2411-001: SDPO consistently delivers high-quality, reward-aligned images across various sampling step settings.
elements.sent-2411-002: All images are generated using the same random seed.. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-003: Aligning diffusion models with downstream objectives is essential for their practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, standard alignment methods often struggle with step generalization when directly applied to few-step diffusion models, leading to inconsistent performance across different denoising step scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address this, we introduce Stepwise Diffusion Policy Optimization (SDPO), a novel alignment method tailored for few-step diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unlike prior approaches that rely on a single sparse reward from only the final step of each denoising trajectory for trajectory-level optimization, SDPO incorporates dense reward feedback at every intermediate step.
elements.sent-2411-007: By learning the differences in dense rewards between paired samples, SDPO facilitates stepwise optimization of few-step diffusion models, ensuring consistent alignment across all denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-008: To promote stable and efficient training, SDPO introduces an online reinforcement learning framework featuring several novel strategies designed to effectively exploit the stepwise granularity of dense rewards.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental results demonstrate that SDPO consistently outperforms prior methods in reward-based alignment across diverse step configurations, underscoring its robust step generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-011: While text-to-image diffusion models~\cite{d2015,sd,sdxl,sd3 excel at generating high-resolution images from text prompts, they often suffer from slow generation speed due to their iterative denoising processes.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Recent approaches~\cite{salimans2022progressive,meng2023on,add,luo2023diff,cm,lcm,ctm,instaflow,lightning,dmd,dmd2,nguyen2024cvpr have shown promise in distilling standard multi-step diffusion models into few-step generators, significantly reducing the number of denoising steps required to produce appealing, high-resolution images.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, these few-step diffusion models may not inherently align with specific downstream objectives, such as aesthetic quality~\cite{aes or user preferences~\cite{imagereward,hpsv2,pick,richhf .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: A promising approach to address this alignment problem for multi-step diffusion models is reinforcement learning (RL)-based finetuning \cite{sftpg,dpok,ddpo , which enables model optimization toward downstream reward functions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, these methods typically assume a fixed number of steps for each denoising trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-016: When applied to finetune models on few-step trajectories (e.g. 1 to 4 steps), they often result in training instability and sample inefficiency.
elements.sent-2411-017: Conversely, using the standard step settings (e.g. 20 to 50 steps) of these methods can lead to step generalization issues, where models overfit to specific trajectory lengths, impairing their few-step inference ability.
elements.sent-2411-018: A key factor in this issue is the sparse reward feedback from only the final denoising step, causing models to overly focus on the final image quality while neglecting the intermediate steps.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, deriving dense rewards for each step is challenging, as existing reward functions are typically based on the distributions of final clean images rather than the intermediate noisy samples.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we propose Stepwise Diffusion Policy Optimization (SDPO), an RL algorithm that aligns few-step diffusion models by learning dense reward differences between paired samples in a stepwise manner.
elements.sent-2411-021: Specifically, SDPO provides continuous, dense reward feedback throughout the entire denoising trajectory by utilizing predicted original samples from each intermediate step, rather than focusing solely on the final output.
elements.sent-2411-022: To obtain this dense reward feedback efficiently, we develop a latent similarity-based reward prediction strategy.
elements.sent-2411-023: This strategy adaptively selects key reward query steps within each trajectory, predicting dense rewards for the remaining steps based on latent similarity, minimizing computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-024: By incorporating dense reward feedback at each denoising step, SDPO distinguishes itself from traditional reward difference-based methods~\cite{prdp,rebel , which typically rely on the difference of final-step rewards for trajectory-level optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Instead, SDPO facilitates stepwise optimization based on the differences of dense rewards, reducing the risk of overfitting to sparse, final-step rewards and promoting consistent alignment across all denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-026: To ensure a stable and efficient optimization process, SDPO introduces an online RL framework featuring several novel strategies, such as per-step-prompt reward normalization, stepwise importance weighting, and step-shuffled gradient updates---each specifically designed to effectively exploit the stepwise granularity of our dense reward predictions.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our experiments show that SDPO demonstrates superior sample efficiency in finetuning few-step diffusion models and exhibits strong step generalization in post-training evaluations, consistently achieving high reward scores and delivering reward-aligned images across diverse sampling step settings.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-029: We propose SDPO, which addresses step generalization in aligning few-step diffusion models for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-030: By incorporating dense reward feedback throughout the denoising process, SDPO facilitates stepwise diffusion policy optimization based on dense reward differences.
elements.sent-2411-031: To efficiently provide dense rewards, we develop a latent similarity-based reward prediction strategy, ensuring dense feedback without excessive computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-032: We design a novel online RL framework for SDPO, with several key innovations that effectively exploit dense rewards, facilitating more stable and efficient optimization.
elements.sent-2411-033: We validate SDPO’s effectiveness in improving step generalization through comprehensive experiments, showing consistent alignment across diverse step configurations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models~\cite{ddpm generate high-quality samples by iteratively transforming noise into complex data distributions through a denoising process.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In text-to-image applications, these models typically condition each denoising step on a text prompt $\mathbf{c}$ that describes the desired image, and are operated in two key phases: a forward process that gradually adds noise to an initial sample $\mathbf{x _0$ at each step $t \in \{1, \dots, T-1\}$, resulting in progressively noisier samples $\mathbf{x _t$; and a reverse process that estimates the conditional distribution $p_\theta(\mathbf{x _{t-1 |\mathbf{x _t, \mathbf{c )$ at each step, iteratively removing noise to recover the original data.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: In the reverse process, each denoising step $t$ produces an estimate of the underlying clean sample $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$, often referred to as the predicted original sample, or the denoised sample.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: At each step $t$, given a noisy input $\mathbf{x _t$, the model predicts the noise component $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _t, t, \mathbf{c )$, which represents the noise present in the current sample.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Using this noise estimate, the model computes the predicted original sample $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$, as: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \left( \mathbf{x}_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t, \mathbf{c}) \right) / \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}, \label{eq:denoising}\end{equation} where $\bar{\alpha _t$ is the cumulative product of the noise scaling factors $\alpha_t$ up to step $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The quality of these intermediate predictions progressively improves as denoising continues.
elements.sent-2411-040: Denoising as a Markov decision process (MDP).
elements.sent-2411-041: To perform RL-based finetuning for diffusion models, prior works~\cite{dpok,ddpo formulate the denoising process of diffusion models as a $T$-step MDP, which is defined as follows: \begin{equation}s_t \triangleq (c, x_{T-t}), \quad a_t \triangleq x_{T-t-1}, \label{eq:mdp1} \pi(a_t | s_t) \triangleq p_\theta(x_{T-t-1} | x_{T-t}, c), \label{eq:mdp2} r(s_t, a_t) \triangleq  R(x_0, c), if\ t = T-1 0, otherwise,  \label{eq:mdp3}\end{equation} where the denoising process $p_\theta$ parameterized by the diffusion model parameters $\theta$ is defined as the policy in this MDP.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}s_t \triangleq (c, x_{T-t}), \quad a_t \triangleq x_{T-t-1}, \label{eq:mdp1} \pi(a_t | s_t) \triangleq p_\theta(x_{T-t-1} | x_{T-t}, c), \label{eq:mdp2} r(s_t, a_t) \triangleq  R(x_0, c), if\ t = T-1 0, otherwise,  \label{eq:mdp3}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-042: Starting with an initial state $\mathbf{x _T$ sampled from the noise distribution, at each step $t$, the policy treats the denoised sample $\mathbf{x _{T-t}$ as the current state $s_t$, and the next state $\mathbf{x _{T-t-1}$ as the action $a_t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Trajectory rewards are defined sparsely, with only the final image $\mathbf{x _0$ being evaluated using the predefined reward function $R(\mathbf{x _0, \mathbf{c )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Then the RL objective for diffusion models is to find the optimal diffusion policy that maximizes the expected reward of final images for prompts sampled from a distribution $p(\mathbf{c )$, i.e., \begin{equation}\min_{\theta} ~ \mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{c})}\mathbb{E}_{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}_0 | \mathbf{c})}\left[ -R(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{c}) \right]. \label{eq:obj1}\end{equation} Optimization via policy gradient.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Framing the denoising process as a diffusion policy enables exact computation of log-likelihoods and their gradients w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-046: Given a full denoising trajectory $\mathbf{x _{0:T}$, we can specifically compute the log-likelihood $\log{p_{\theta (\mathbf{x _{t-1 | \mathbf{x _t, \mathbf{c )}$ for each denoising step.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This allows for the computation of the log-likelihood gradient $\nabla_\theta \log{p_{\theta (\mathbf{x _{t-1 | \mathbf{x _t, \mathbf{c )}$, which captures the sensitivity of each transition probability to changes in the model parameters.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: By exploiting this property, prior works such as DPOK~\cite{dpok and DDPO~\cite{ddpo apply policy gradient methods~\cite{reinforce,ppo to estimate the gradients of \cref{eq:obj1 as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{c})}\mathbb{E}_{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{0:T} | \mathbf{c})}\left[ -R(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{c}) \sum_{t=1}^T \nabla_\theta \log{p_{\theta} (\mathbf{x}_{t-1} | \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{c})} \right]. \label{eq:obj2}\end{equation} Reward difference learning.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Inspired by direct preference optimization (DPO)~\cite{dpo that converts the RL objective for language models into a supervised classification objective, recent approaches~\cite{prdp,rebel further derive a mean squared error-based objective for diffusion models, where the goal is to match the differences in log-likelihood ratios with the differences in rewards across paired denoising trajectories: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}^a, \mathbf{x}^b, \mathbf{c}} \Bigg[ \sum_{t=1}^T \bigg( \frac{1}{\eta} \left( \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^a_t | \mathbf{c})}{p_{\theta'}(\mathbf{x}^a_t | \mathbf{c})} - \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^b_t | \mathbf{c})}{p_{\theta'}(\mathbf{x}^b_t | \mathbf{c})} \right) \bigg. \Bigg. \Bigg. \bigg. - \left( R(\mathbf{x}^a_0, \mathbf{c}) - R(\mathbf{x}^b_0, \mathbf{c}) \right) \bigg)^2 \Bigg]. \end{aligned} \label{eq:obj3}\end{equation} Here, $\mathbf{x ^a_t$ and $\mathbf{x ^b_t$ represent paired samples from the denoising step $t$ of two independent trajectories, both generated using the same text prompt $\mathbf{c}$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation} E_{x^a, x^b, c} \Bigg[ \sum_{t=1}^T \bigg( \frac{1}{\eta} \left( \log \frac{p_\theta(x^a_t | c)}{p_{\theta'}(x^a_t | c)} - \log \frac{p_\theta(x^b_t | c)}{p_{\theta'}(x^b_t | c)} \right) \bigg. \Bigg. \Bigg. \bigg. - \left( R(x^a_0, c) - R(x^b_0, c) \right) \bigg)^2 \Bigg].  \label{eq:obj3}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-050: These paired samples are used to compute the differences in their corresponding rewards, \ie $R(\mathbf{x ^a_0, \mathbf{c ) - R(\mathbf{x ^b_0, \mathbf{c )$, as well as the differences in their log-likelihood ratios.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In particular, $\log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x ^a_t | \mathbf{c ) {p_{\theta' (\mathbf{x ^a_t | \mathbf{c )}$ denotes the log-likelihood ratio of sample $\mathbf{x ^a_t$ under the current model parameters $\theta$ compared to the previous or reference model parameters $\theta'$, helping to quantify the change in the model during online optimization.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Furthermore, the log-ratio scale factor $\eta$ is introduced to balance the contribution of log-ratio differences relative to reward differences in the objective, ensuring that neither term dominates the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-053: Empirically, both Deng \etal~\cite{prdp and Gao \etal~\cite{rebel demonstrate that this reward difference-based approach results in more stable reward optimization for diffusion models, compared to vanilla policy gradient methods~\cite{dpok,ddpo .}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/sdpo.pdf} \caption{SDPO framework. SDPO begins by sampling two denoised trajectories, $\{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{T-1}^a, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{T-1}^b,\dots, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_0^a, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_0^b\}$, for reward estimation, alongside two corresponding noisy trajectories, $\{\mathbf{x}_{T-1}^a, \mathbf{x}_{T-1}^b, \dots, \mathbf{x}_0^a, \mathbf{x}_0^b\}$, for computing the log-ratio $\tilde{\rho}_t$, using a shared prompt $c$ and initial noise $\mathbf{x}_T$. For each denoised trajectory, the reward function $R$ is queried only at the first ($t = T - 1$), final ($t = 0$), and an anchor step ($t_{\text{anchor}}$) adaptively selected based on cosine similarity. Additionally, cosine similarity is also applied to interpolate the dense reward $\hat{R}_t$ for each step of the trajectories, informing the advantage estimate $\hat{A}_t$. Finally, for each shuffled step $\tau_t$, we compute the MSE loss between the advantage difference $\Delta\hat{A}_{\tau_t}$ and the log-ratio difference $\Delta\tilde{\rho}_{\tau_t}$, weighted by $\lambda^{(T-\tau_t-1)}/\eta$, thus enabling stepwise policy updates.} \label{fig:sdpo}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Rethinking RL-based Finetuning of Few-Step Diffusion Models
elements.sent-2411-054: To achieve RL-based finetuning of few-step diffusion models, we first consider three intuitive strategies:.
elements.sent-2411-055: The first strategy involves training on short denoising trajectories (e.g. 1 or 2 steps).
elements.sent-2411-056: While it aligns with the few-step objective, this approach often leads to training instability and sample inefficiency, as detailed in \cref{appendix:instability .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The second strategy adopts the standard step settings (e.g. 50 steps) from prior works~\cite{dpok,ddpo , enabling more comprehensive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, this approach risks overfitting to specific trajectory lengths, thereby compromising the model's ability to generalize during inference with fewer steps, as shown in our experimental results in \cref{sec:4.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: The third strategy employs trajectories of varying lengths, which introduces diversity and may improve step generalization.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, the increased variance across trajectories can also lead to instability during training.
elements.sent-2411-061: To improve step generalization while maintaining training stability, we propose to incorporate dense reward feedback throughout the entire denoising trajectory, rather than focusing solely on the sparse, final-step rewards.
elements.sent-2411-062: As introduced earlier in \cref{sec:2.1 , the predicted original sample $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$ serves as the model’s intermediate estimate of the final, noise-free data at the corresponding denoising step $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In few-step diffusion models, the accuracy of the predicted $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$ at each denoising step $t$ improves significantly, allowing even early iterations of $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$ to be used as reasonable approximations of the final generated sample.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Remarkably, advanced few-step models such as SDXL-Turbo~\cite{add enable high-quality single-step sampling, where the first prediction $\hat{\mathbf{x _{T-1}$ can already serve as a plausible generated output, with subsequent steps progressively refining the sample.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Accordingly, we can utilize the predicted $\hat{\mathbf{x ^t$ from each intermediate denoising step $t$ to provide a dense, stepwise reward feedback.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: To achieve this, we refine the denoising MDP definition from \cref{sec:2.2 by applying the predefined reward function to each predicted $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$, yielding the updated formulation of \cref{eq:mdp3 as follows: \begin{equation}r(s_t, a_t) \triangleq R(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t, \mathbf{c}), \label{eq:mdp4}\end{equation} where each predicted $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$ can serve as a surrogate for the final output of a full $t$-step denoising process.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: This dense reward feedback facilitates policy optimization at each intermediate step, introducing diversity into training with a fixed trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-069: Although variance still exists in different steps, it can be easily managed through reward normalization due to the consistency across trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-070: Ultimately, this stepwise optimization guides the model to progressively refine intermediate samples via dense rewards, striking a balance between generalization and stability in RL finetuning.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Dense Reward Difference Learning for Few-Step Diffusion Models
elements.sent-2411-071: In this section, we present our approach to dense reward difference learning for few-step diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-072: While dense rewards provide continuous feedback throughout the denoising process, directly querying rewards at each intermediate step is computationally intensive, as it requires decoding intermediate predictions $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$ from latent space to image space.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: To address this, we propose an efficient dense reward prediction method that leverages latent similarity, reducing reward queries while maintaining robust and granular guidance throughout the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-074: Dense reward prediction via latent similarity.
elements.sent-2411-075: Rather than querying rewards at every denoising step, we limit reward queries to three per trajectory: two fixed queries at the first and last steps, and one query at an adaptively selected intermediate step, referred to as the anchor step.
elements.sent-2411-076: To determine the anchor step, we first need to calculate the cosine similarity between the latent representations of each intermediate $\hat{\mathbf{x _t$ and those at the first and last steps, respectively: \begin{equation}\text{Sim}_{\text{first}}^t = \frac{\langle \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{T-1} \rangle}{\|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t\| \|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{T-1}\|}, \quad \text{Sim}_{\text{last}}^t = \frac{\langle \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_0 \rangle}{\|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t\| \|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0\|}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Then, the intermediate step that minimizes the sum of cosine similarities to the first and last steps is selected as the anchor step $t_{\text{anchor }$, as it represents the step with the highest divergence from the two extremes of the trajectory: \begin{equation}t_{\text{anchor}} = \underset{t \in \{1, \dots, T-2\}}{\arg\min} \left( \text{Sim}_{\text{first}}^t + \text{Sim}_{\text{last}}^t \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Once the anchor step $t_{\text{anchor }$ is identified, we further compute the cosine similarity between each intermediate step and the anchor step.}
elements.sent-2411-079: This results in three cosine similarity values for each intermediate step $t$: (1) similarity to the first step, $\text{Sim _{\text{first ^t$, (2) similarity to the anchor step, $\text{Sim _{\text{anchor ^t$, and (3) similarity to the last step, $\text{Sim _{\text{last ^t$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: These three similarity values are then used to perform a weighted averaging of the three reward queries, i.e., $R(\hat{\mathbf{x _{T-1 , \mathbf{c )$, $R(\hat{\mathbf{x _{t_{\text{anchor , \mathbf{c )$, and $R(\hat{\mathbf{x _0, \mathbf{c )$ (abbr.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Leveraging this dense reward prediction method, we can now refine the reward difference learning approach, introduced in \cref{sec:2.2 , into a more granular manner.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, the objective is to match the difference in log-likelihood ratios, $\rho^j_t(\theta) := \log (p_\theta(\mathbf{x ^j_t | \mathbf{c )/p_{\theta' (\mathbf{x ^j_t | \mathbf{c ))$, with the difference in dense rewards $\hat{R _t^i$ at each step $t$ for the paired trajectories $j \in \{a, b\}$: \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \Bigg[ \left( \frac{1}{\eta} \left( \rho^a_t(\theta) - \rho^b_t(\theta) \right) - \left( \hat{R}_t^a - \hat{R}_t^b \right) \right)^2 \Bigg]. \label{eq:obj4}\end{equation} In contrast to the trajectory-level objective in \cref{eq:obj3 , this stepwise objective enables more frequent model updates by eliminating the need to accumulate full trajectory data, thereby significantly improving sample efficiency.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In this section, we present Stepwise Diffusion Policy Optimization (SDPO), an online RL algorithm tailored for stable, efficient optimization with dense reward difference learning.
elements.sent-2411-084: Building on our established studies from \cref{sec:3.1,sec:3.2 , SDPO introduces several key innovations that effectively exploit the stepwise granularity of our dense reward predictions, further enhancing sample efficiency and step generalization in finetuning few-step diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-085: To effectively facilitate optimization in a stepwise manner, it is crucial to incorporate feedback from other steps into the evaluation of the current step.
elements.sent-2411-086: In the online RL paradigm, although the log-likelihood ratios for other steps cannot be directly computed during each iteration of stepwise optimization, the dense rewards for the full trajectory are available from the sampling stage.
elements.sent-2411-087: Leveraging the availability of future feedback from dense rewards, SDPO calculates a cumulative return $\hat{G _t$ for each dense reward prediction $\hat{R _t$ with a discount factor $\gamma \in \left(0, 1\right]$, as shown below: \begin{equation}\hat{G}_t = \sum_{k=0}^{t} \gamma^{k} \hat{R}_{t-k}. \label{eq:return}\end{equation} Per-step-prompt reward normalization.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: To reduce variance across different prompts, DDPO~\cite{ddpo applies a per-prompt normalization strategy, normalizing rewards by maintaining a running mean and standard deviation for each prompt individually.}
elements.sent-2411-089: However, we aim to address not only the variance across prompts but also the variance across different denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus, we normalize the discounted return $\hat{G _t$ from \cref{eq:return on a per-step-prompt basis, tracking running statistics independently for each step-prompt pair.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: This yields advantage estimates, $\hat{A _t^a$ and $\hat{A _t^b$, at each step of both trajectories in \cref{eq:obj4 , substituting $\hat{R _t^a$ and $\hat{R _t^b$.. Stepwise importance weighting.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Inspired by Yang \etal~\cite{spo , we facilitate reward optimization for foundational image patterns in early denoising steps by applying stepwise importance weighting with a decay rate $\lambda$, to the log-likelihood ratios in our dense reward difference learning objective.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Specifically, the stepwise importance weight follows an exponentially decaying pattern from the first step $t=T-1$ to the last step $t=0$, defined as $\lambda^{(T-t-1)}$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Thus, the objective becomes matching the log-ratio difference, $\Delta\rho_t(\theta) := \rho^a_t(\theta) - \rho^b_t(\theta)$, weighted by $\lambda^{(T-t-1) /\eta$, with the corresponding advantage difference, $\Delta\hat{A _t := \hat{A _t^a - \hat{A _t^b$, at each step $t$ in paired trajectories: \begin{equation}l_\theta = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \left[ \left( \Delta\rho_t(\theta) \cdotp \lambda^{(T-t-1)}/\eta - \Delta\hat{A}_t \right)^2 \right] \label{eq:obj5}\end{equation} Additionally, to prevent large, unstable policy updates during online optimization, we constrain the log-likelihood ratios within a trust region by clipping them at a threshold $\epsilon$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Defining the clipped ratios as $\tilde{\rho ^j_t(\theta) := \text{clip (\rho^j_t(\theta), -\epsilon, \epsilon)$, we can express the clipped objective as follows: \begin{equation}\tilde{l}_\theta = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \left[ \left( \Delta\tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \cdotp \lambda^{(T-t-1)}/\eta - \Delta\hat{A}_t \right)^2 \right] \label{eq:obj6}\end{equation}.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Stepwise Diffusion Policy Optimization} \label{alg:SDPO} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: Pretrained diffusion model $p_\theta$, prompt distribution $p(\mathbf{c})$, number of training epochs $E$, number of denoising steps per trajectory $T$, batch size $B$ \FOR{epoch $e = 1, \dots, E$} \STATE Store the previous model $p_\theta' \leftarrow p_\theta$ \STATE Sample prompts $\{ \mathbf{c}_i \sim p(\mathbf{c}) \}_{i=1}^{B}$ \STATE Generate trajectories $\{ (\mathbf{x}^{a,i}, \mathbf{x}^{b,i}) \sim p_{\theta'}(\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{c}_i) \}_{i=1}^{B}$ \FOR{batch $i = 1, \dots, B$} \STATE Compute rewards $\{\hat{R}_t^{a,i}, \hat{R}_t^{b,i}\}_{t=0}^{T-1}$ via \cref{eq:dense} \STATE Compute returns $\{\hat{G}_t^{a,i}, \hat{G}_t^{b,i}\}_{t=0}^{T-1}$ via \cref{eq:return} \STATE Normalize returns per step-prompt pair, and get advantage estimates $\{\hat{A}_t^{a,i}, \hat{A}_t^{b,i}\}_{t=0}^{T-1}$ \STATE Generate shuffled step indices $\{\tau_t^i\}_{t=0}^{T-1}$ \ENDFOR \FOR{step $t = 1, \dots, T$} \STATE Update the current model $p_\theta$ via \cref{eq:update} \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-097: To implement stepwise gradient updates for \cref{eq:obj5 , SDPO incorporates a novel step-shuffled update strategy, where each gradient update selects data from a single step in each independently shuffled trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-098: In each training epoch, SDPO performs $T$ updates, corresponding to the number of steps per trajectory, with training samples divided into $B$ batches, each containing a pair of trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-099: To avoid overfitting to specific denoising steps, all trajectories are independently shuffled along the step dimension within each batch.
elements.sent-2411-100: Let $\tau_i$ represent the shuffled step indices for the $i$-th batch, then the $t$-th gradient update for $\theta$ is formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_t(\theta) \leftarrow \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \max \left(l_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{a,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{x}^{b,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{c}_i), \tilde{l}_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{a,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{x}^{b,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{c}_i) \right), \label{eq:update}\end{equation} where we select the maximum value between the unclipped objective $l_\theta$ and the clipped objective $\tilde{l _\theta$, both calculated using samples $(\mathbf{x ^{a,i _{\tau_t^i , \mathbf{x ^{b,i _{\tau_t^i , \mathbf{c _i)$ from the $\tau_t^i$-th step of the paired trajectories in the $i$-th batch.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: The pseudo-code of SDPO is in \cref{alg:SDPO , with the overall framework shown in \cref{fig:sdpo .}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.325\textwidth]{figures/aes_50s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.325\textwidth]{figures/img_50s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/hps_50s.pdf} \caption{Learning curves by sample efficiency. Reward scores (left: Aesthetic Score, middle: ImageReward, right: HPSv2) are evaluated for images generated with 50 denoising steps. The horizontal axis represents the cumulative number of training samples.} \label{fig:50s}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.325\textwidth]{figures/aes_1s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.32\textwidth]{figures/img_1s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.325\textwidth]{figures/hps_1s.pdf} \caption{Evaluation curves for generalization to 1-step. Reward scores are evaluated for images generated with a single denoising step.} \label{fig:1s}
elements.sent-2411-102: In this section, we present comprehensive experiments to validate the efficacy of our SDPO in finetuning few-step diffusion models with various reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-103: In our main experiments (\cref{sec:4.2,sec:4.3 ), we use SD-Turbo~\cite{add , a few-step text-to-image model distilled from Stable Diffusion v2.1~\cite{sd through adversarial diffusion distillation~\cite{add , as the base model.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: For efficient finetuning, we apply Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)~\cite{lora , which enables updating only the LoRA weights while freezing the pretrained model parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Following DDPO~\cite{ddpo , we generate denoising trajectories using the DDIM sampler~\cite{ddim with 50 denoising steps and a prompt set of 45 animal names.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Since SD-Turbo does not support classifier-free guidance or negative prompts, we disable them by setting the guidance scale to $1.0$.
elements.sent-2411-107: The model is finetuned separately with four commonly used reward functions: Aesthetic Score~\cite{aes , ImageReward~\cite{imagereward , HPSv2~\cite{hpsv2 , and PickScore~\cite{pick .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: We compare the reward optimization performance of SDPO with DDPO~\cite{ddpo , REBEL~\cite{rebel , and D3PO~\cite{d3po , each exemplifying a distinct approach to diffusion alignment: policy gradient methods, reward difference-based methods, and DPO-style methods, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: As each of these methods was originally implemented for different diffusion model variations, we reconfigure them to operate consistently on SD-Turbo.
elements.sent-2411-110: For a fair comparison, we standardize key hyperparameters across all algorithms, such as denoising steps per trajectory ($T=50$) and training samples per epoch.
elements.sent-2411-112: We prioritize sample efficiency as a key metric for evaluating reward optimization performance, examining how effectively each algorithm utilizes generated samples to improve reward outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-113: Learning curves in \cref{fig:50s illustrate the sample efficiency of each algorithm during reward optimization with 50-step denoising across different reward functions.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The horizontal axis specifies the cumulative number of generated image samples utilized for reward optimization, while the vertical axis reflects the average reward values calculated from these samples.
elements.sent-2411-115: In \cref{fig:50s , the steeper learning curves of SDPO suggest that our method is able to achieve higher reward scores with relatively fewer samples compared to others, indicating its superior sample efficiency.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/dr_1s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.32\textwidth]{figures/dr_1s_time.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/gam_1s.pdf} \caption{Ablation study on dense reward prediction (left \& middle) and discounted returns (right) for SDPO.} \label{fig:ablation1}

elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Generalization to unseen prompts. 1s, 4s, 16s, and 50s indicate 1-step, 4-step, 16-step, and 50-step denoising, respectively.} \label{tab:unseen} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lcccccccccccccccccccccc} \multirow{2}{*}{ Method} \multicolumn{4}{c}{ImageReward} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Aesthetic Score} \multicolumn{4}{c}{HPSv2} \multicolumn{4}{c}{PickScore} \cmidrule(lr){2-5} \cmidrule(lr){6-9} \cmidrule(lr){10-13} \cmidrule(lr){14-17} 1s 4s 16s 50s 1s 4s 16s 50s 1s 4s 16s 50s 1s 4s 16s 50s SD-Turbo~\cite{add} 0.985 0.927 1.063 0.392 6.049 5.920 5.820 5.432 27.83 27.96 28.34 27.40 22.60 22.65 22.70 21.78 D3PO~\cite{d3po} 1.152 1.009 1.196 0.801 6.285 6.167 6.102 5.958 28.06 28.21 28.65 28.19 22.59 22.65 22.79 22.36 DDPO~\cite{ddpo} 1.191 1.084 1.155 1.088 6.610 6.619 6.604 6.733 28.48 28.50 28.59 28.73 22.53 22.64 22.87 22.81 REBEL~\cite{rebel} 1.139 1.128 1.189 0.918 6.487 6.411 6.329 6.245 28.33 28.38 28.70 28.79 22.48 22.54 22.74 22.73 \rowcolor{blue!10} SDPO (Ours) 1.609 1.576 1.556 1.573 7.077 7.333 7.802 8.175 28.77 28.87 29.23 29.26 22.80 22.94 23.46 23.86 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-116: We evaluate the step generalization capabilities of models finetuned on 50-step denoising trajectories during inference with fewer denoising steps, where samples are generated using varying number of denoising steps (1, 2, 4, 8, and 16).
elements.sent-2411-117: Specifically, we leverage each intermediate model checkpoint to generate samples with different step configurations, subsequently evaluating these samples across various reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-118: Across all evaluations, our SDPO consistently achieves the highest reward scores compared to other algorithms, indicating superior step generalization.
elements.sent-2411-120: We further evaluate finetuned models on unseen prompts from \cite{dpok , which specify attributes like color (A green colored rabbit), count (Four wolves in the park), composition (A cat and a dog), and location (A dog on the moon).}
elements.sent-2411-121: Across all these evaluations, our SDPO continues to outperform the pretrained SD-Turbo and the other algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-122: We also present qualitative comparisons of generated images for unseen prompts in \cref{fig:teaser and \cref{appendix:vis , where SDPO consistently delivers reward-aligned images across all steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: We conduct a series of ablation studies to systematically analyze the impact of each key component in our SDPO on its generalization performance to reward evaluations for 1-step sampling.
elements.sent-2411-124: Notably, we optimize Aesthetic Score in these studies, as it lacks supervision for text-image alignment, presenting a greater challenge for achieving generalization.
elements.sent-2411-126: As detailed in \cref{sec:3.2 , we use 3 reward queries per trajectory, latent similarity, and an adaptively selected anchor to efficiently predict dense rewards.}
elements.sent-2411-127: To examine the effectiveness of this approach, we compare SDPO with variations that employ alternative strategies for dense reward prediction, including:.
elements.sent-2411-128: The left plot in \cref{fig:ablation1 compares the generalization performance of SDPO variations, using 3 and 50 reward queries, by evaluating rewards from 1-step sampling during training.}
elements.sent-2411-129: The middle plot in \cref{fig:ablation1 evaluates the training time efficiency of different dense reward prediction methods, using GPU hours on the x-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Our proposed method consistently outperforms all the variations, demonstrating its effectiveness in predicting dense rewards.
elements.sent-2411-131: Notably, while the variation with 50 reward queries demonstrates comparable sample efficiency to the original method, it necessitates substantially more computational time.
elements.sent-2411-132: In contrast, our approach not only achieves high sample efficiency but also significantly enhances computational efficiency, due to its superior effectiveness in addressing step generalization.
elements.sent-2411-134: We then evaluate SDPO with different discount factors: $\gamma = 0.99$, $0.9$, and $1$, along with a variation that directly uses rewards without returns.
elements.sent-2411-135: As shown in the right plot of \cref{fig:ablation1 , SDPO with $\gamma = 0.99$ achieves the best performance in step generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-137: We also compare the per-step-prompt normalization strategy in SDPO with two alternatives: (1) per-prompt normalization, which normalizes rewards on a per prompt basis, and (2) global normalization, which simply normalizes rewards across all samples.
elements.sent-2411-138: The left plot in \cref{fig:ablation2 shows both alternatives underperform our per-step-prompt normalization.}
elements.sent-2411-140: We further analyze the impact of stepwise importance weighting in SDPO with different decay factors: $\lambda = 0.99$, $0.9$, and $1$, as well as a variation that applies weights in a reverse order.
elements.sent-2411-141: As shown in the middle plot of \cref{fig:ablation2 , SDPO with $\lambda = 0.99$ achieves the best step generalization performance.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Finally, we evaluate the impact of replacing step-shuffled updates in SDPO with two alternative strategies: (1) step-accumulated updates and (2) stepwise updates without shuffling.
elements.sent-2411-144: The right plot in \cref{fig:ablation2 shows both alternatives underperform our step-shuffled updates.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Step-accumulated updates, by accumulating gradients over all denoising steps, lead to a significant decay in sample efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-146: Meanwhile, stepwise updates without shuffling demonstrate poor step generalization, likely due to overfitting to the fixed step order.
elements.sent-2411-147: We further explore finetuning consistency models with our SDPO by utilizing the RLCM baseline~\cite{rlcm , which applies both DDPO and REBEL for finetuning a latent consistency model~\cite{lcm distilled from Dreamshaper v7~\cite{dreamshaper7 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:rlcm , SDPO still outperforms DDPO and REBEL in reward optimization for the latent consistency model.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Existing approaches for diffusion alignment~\cite{lee2023,hps,liu2024alignment can be broadly classified into two categories: (1) reward-based methods, which use techniques such as policy gradient-based RL finetuning~\cite{dpok,ddpo,hao2024optimizing,tdpo,zhang2024large,uehara2024feedback,lee2024parrot,chen2024enhancing,mo2024dynamic , reward difference~\cite{prdp,rebel , or reward backpropagation through sampling~\cite{draft,alignprop,prabhudesai2024video,wu2024deep,li2024textcraftor to optimize the model based on explicit reward functions; and (2) preference-based methods~\cite{d3po,wallace2024diffusion,croitoru2024curriculum,yang24a,li2024aligning,yuan2024self,gu2024diffusion,spo,hong2024margin,karthik2024scalable , which employ DPO-style objectives to directly align the model with data-driven preferences.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Our work is motivated by the reward difference methods.
elements.sent-2411-152: Despite their efficacy in aligning multi-step diffusion models, they often fail in direct applications to few-step diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-154: Recent approaches~\cite{li2024reward,ren2024hyper incorporate reward guidance into consistency distillation, while not directly applicable to other distilled few-step models.}
elements.sent-2411-155: RLCM~\cite{rlcm introduces an RL finetuning framework specifically for consistency models, which we use as a baseline in our extensive analysis of applying our SDPO to consistency models.}
elements.sent-2411-156: In contrast, our work extends RL finetuning to arbitrary few-step diffusion models, while addressing the step generalization problem.
elements.sent-2411-158: Recent studies have explored dense feedback in diffusion alignment.
elements.sent-2411-159: For RL finetuning, Zhang \etal~\cite{tdpo highlight the importance of dense rewards to prevent overoptimization, and introduce a temporal critic to estimate dense rewards.}
elements.sent-2411-160: For preference learning, Yang \etal~\cite{yang24a apply temporal discounting in a DPO-style objective to distinguish preferences at each step, while Liang \etal~\cite{spo build a step-aware preference model to provide stepwise preference.}}
elements.sent-2411-161: In contrast, our work incorporates dense rewards into RL finetuning of few-step diffusion models without requiring additional model training.
elements.sent-2411-162: This paper presented SDPO, an RL framework that aligns few-step diffusion models with downstream objectives via dense reward difference learning.
elements.sent-2411-163: By leveraging our dense reward predictions alongside novel strategies designed to exploit their stepwise granularity, SDPO effectively addresses the step generalization problem.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our experiments demonstrate that SDPO achieves robust diffusion alignment across diverse step configurations, showcasing its potential for practical applications of few-step diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-165: For our main experiments in \cref{sec:4.2 , we use SD-Turbo~\cite{add as the base model to compare the performance of DDPO~\cite{ddpo , REBEL~\cite{rebel , D3PO~\cite{d3po , and our proposed SDPO in finetuning few-step diffusion models.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Detailed implementation for each of these methods is provided below: DDPO implementation.
elements.sent-2411-167: We use the official PyTorch codebase of DDPO, substituting SD-Turbo as the base model.
elements.sent-2411-168: Since SD-Turbo does not support classifier-free guidance or negative prompts, we disable them by setting the guidance scale to $1.0$.. REBEL implementation.
elements.sent-2411-169: As REBEL was originally implemented to finetune consistency models~\cite{cm , we adapt it to finetune SD-Turbo by using the DDPO codebase.}
elements.sent-2411-170: For a fair comparison, we keep the shared hyperparameters---such as denoising steps per trajectory, classifier-free guidance scale, learning rate, batch size, and running statistics settings---consistent with DDPO, with REBEL-specific settings derived from the REBEL paper.
elements.sent-2411-171: Unlike DDPO, which generates only one trajectory per batch, REBEL produces a pair of trajectories for reward difference learning.
elements.sent-2411-172: To ensure fair sample rollouts, we halve the number of batches and gradient accumulation steps per epoch for REBEL.. D3PO implementation.
elements.sent-2411-173: Applying D3PO to finetune SD-Turbo is relatively straightforward, as the official D3PO codebase is also built on DDPO.
elements.sent-2411-174: However, for a fair comparison, we still reconfigure certain hyperparameters to align with our DDPO implementation, including denoising steps per trajectory, classifier-free guidance scale, learning rate, and batch size.
elements.sent-2411-175: Like REBEL, D3PO also generates a pair of trajectories per batch for preference learning.
elements.sent-2411-176: Thus, we maintain the same number of batches and gradient accumulation steps per epoch for D3PO as we do for REBEL.. SDPO implementation.
elements.sent-2411-177: Our SDPO setup retains most shared hyperparameters from the REBEL configuration.
elements.sent-2411-178: By leveraging dense rewards, our SDPO removes the need to accumulate full trajectory data, enabling more frequent model updates.
elements.sent-2411-179: To stabilize training under these frequent updates, we reduce the learning rate for our SDPO.
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{SDPO training hyperparameters for Aesthetic Score, ImageReward, HPSv2, and PickScore.} \label{table:hyper} \begin{tabular}{l|c|cccc} Hyperparameter Notation Aesthetic Score ImageReward HPSv2 PickScore Random seed -- $42$ $42$ $42$ $42$ Denoising steps per trajectory $T$ $50$ $50$ $50$ $50$ Classifier-free guidance scale -- $1.0$ $1.0$ $1.0$ $1.0$ Maximum gradient norm -- $1.0$ $1.0$ $1.0$ $1.0$ Optimizer type -- AdamW AdamW AdamW AdamW Optimizer weight decay -- $1\times10^{-4}$ $1\times10^{-4}$ $1\times10^{-4}$ $1\times10^{-4}$ Optimizer $\beta_1$ -- $0.9$ $0.9$ $0.9$ $0.9$ Optimizer $\beta_2$ -- $0.999$ $0.999$ $0.999$ $0.999$ Optimizer epsilon -- $1\times10^{-8}$ $1\times10^{-8}$ $1\times10^{-8}$ $1\times10^{-8}$ Learning rate -- $1\times10^{-4}$ $5\times10^{-5}$ $5\times10^{-5}$ $6\times10^{-5}$ Sampling batch size per GPU $B_1$ $8$ $8$ $8$ $8$ Sampling batches per epoch -- $4$ $4$ $4$ $4$ Running statistics buffer size -- $32$ $32$ $32$ $32$ Running statistics minimal count -- $16$ $16$ $16$ $16$ Training batch size per GPU $B_2$ $2$ $1$ $1$ $1$ Gradient accumulation steps -- $8$ $32$ $32$ $16$ Log-ratio scale factor $\eta$ $1.0$ $1.0$ $1.0$ $1.0$ Clipping range $\epsilon$ $1\times10^{-4}$ $1\times10^{-4}$ $1\times10^{-4}$ $1\times10^{-4}$ Discount factor $\gamma$ $0.99$ $0.99$ $0.99$ $0.99$ Decay rate $\lambda$ $0.99$ $0.99$ $0.99$ $0.99$ \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-180: Our main experiments in \cref{sec:4.2,sec:4.3 were conducted using the following hardware and software environments:.}
elements.sent-2411-182: In \cref{sec:4 , we utilized a set of 45 animal names as training prompts, listed as follows:.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{tabular}{llllllllll} cat dog horse monkey rabbit zebra spider bird sheep deer cow goat lion tiger bear raccoon fox wolf lizard beetle ant butterfly fish shark whale dolphin squirrel mouse rat snake turtle frog chicken duck goose bee pig turkey fly llama camel bat gorilla hedgehog kangaroo \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-183: For finetuning the latent consistency model~\cite{lcm , we utilize RLCM~\cite{rlcm as the baseline.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: As the official RLCM codebase integrates both DDPO and REBEL, we adapt only our SDPO to it, aligning key hyperparameters with those of REBEL, except for the SDPO-specific ones (e.g., discount factor, decay rate, and clipping range), which follow the settings in \cref{table:hyper .}
elements.section-2411-008: Instability of Prior Methods in Finetuning with Few-Step Trajectories
elements.sent-2411-185: Building on the discussion in \cref{sec:3.1 , we further analyze the instability that arises when using prior methods (DDPO~\cite{ddpo , REBEL~\cite{rebel , and D3PO~\cite{d3po ) to finetune SD-Turbo with denoising trajectories that contain extremely few steps (e.g. 1 or 2 steps).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Specifically, each of these methods struggles to maintain stable reward optimization in these scenarios, with some exhibiting significant reward collapse due to the diminished quality of low-step samples.
elements.sent-2411-187: Therefore, relying solely on low-quality, low-step samples for finetuning is less informative and can lead to suboptimal updates, reducing the overall effectiveness and stability of the training process.
elements.sent-2411-188: Additionally, \Cref{fig:instab also shows the learning curves of DDPO when mixing trajectories of varying lengths during training, where the increased variance across trajectories further contributes to instability.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/instab_1s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/instab_2s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/instab_ms.pdf} \caption{Analysis of the instability of prior methods in finetuning with few-step trajectories. We present the learning curves of DDPO, REBEL, and D3PO during finetuning SD-Turbo with 1-step (left) and 2-step (middle) denoising trajectories, as well as the learning curves of DDPO during finetuning with mixed-step trajectories (right).} \label{fig:instab}
elements.sent-2411-189: Instead of using mixed-length trajectories with high variance and few-step trajectories with low-quality samples, our SDPO utilizes high-quality intermediate samples from longer, uniform-length trajectories as reliable references.
elements.sent-2411-190: By incorporating dense reward feedback, it ensures robust and stable reward optimization across all denoising steps.
elements.section-2411-009: Analysis of Similarity Between Predicted and Target Rewards
elements.sent-2411-191: To further validate the effectiveness of our dense reward prediction approach, we analyze the similarity between the dense rewards predicted by our method, the alternative strategies introduced in our ablation study (\cref{sec:4.3 ), and the reward signals directly computed from the target reward function across full trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-192: In \cref{table:reward , we report three metrics---cosine similarity, L1 distance, and L2 distance---to evaluate the similarity between predicted and target rewards across the following reward functions: Aesthetic Score~\cite{aes , ImageReward~\cite{imagereward , HPSv2~\cite{hpsv2 , and PickScore~\cite{pick .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: To account for variations in the scale of different reward functions, all reward scores are normalized before similarity computation.
elements.sent-2411-194: Compared with the alternative strategies, our approach (3 queries \& similarity \& adaptive anchor) consistently achieves higher cosine similarity and lower L1/L2 distances across all reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-195: This highlights the effectiveness of our dense reward predictions in approximating the target reward signals, further demonstrating its potential for more precise and reliable model optimization.
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Similarity between predicted and target rewards.} \label{table:reward} \begin{tabular}{l l c c c} Reward Function Method Cosine Similarity $\uparrow$ L1 Distance $\downarrow$ L2 Distance $\downarrow$ \multirow{6}{*}{Aesthetic Score} 1 query \& copy $2\times10^{-8}$ $11630$ $131$ 2 queries \& interpolation $0.3368$ $11228$ $130$ 2 queries \& similarity $0.3861$ $9771$ $125$ 3 queries \& similarity \& fixed anchor $0.3654$ $9832$ $127$ 3 queries \& similarity \& random anchor $0.3561$ $9744$ $128$ \rowcolor{blue!10} 3 queries \& similarity \& adaptive anchor $\mathbf{0.4463}$ $\mathbf{9073}$ $\mathbf{119}$ \multirow{6}{*}{ImageReward} 1 query \& copy $2\times10^{-8}$ $11262$ $130$ 2 queries \& interpolation $0.3901$ $10561$ $125$ 2 queries \& similarity $0.4078$ $9340$ $123$ 3 queries \& similarity \& fixed anchor $0.3611$ $9423$ $128$ 3 queries \& similarity \& random anchor $0.3924$ $9337$ $125$ \rowcolor{blue!10} 3 queries \& similarity \& adaptive anchor $\mathbf{0.4683}$ $\mathbf{8598}$ $\mathbf{117}$ \multirow{6}{*}{HPSv2} 1 query \& copy $3\times10^{-8}$ $11411$ $129$ 2 queries \& interpolation $0.3696$ $10798$ $127$ 2 queries \& similarity $0.3676$ $9915$ $127$ 3 queries \& similarity \& fixed anchor $0.3253$ $9886$ $131$ 3 queries \& similarity \& random anchor $0.3648$ $9955$ $128$ \rowcolor{blue!10} 3 queries \& similarity \& adaptive anchor $\mathbf{0.4549}$ $\mathbf{9009}$ $\mathbf{118}$ \multirow{6}{*}{PickScore} 1 query \& copy $3\times10^{-8}$ $11540$ $131$ 2 queries \& interpolation $0.4307$ $10237$ $121$ 2 queries \& similarity $0.4117$ $9318$ $123$ 3 queries \& similarity \& fixed anchor $0.3933$ $9368$ $125$ 3 queries \& similarity \& random anchor $0.3846$ $9324$ $126$ \rowcolor{blue!10} 3 queries \& similarity \& adaptive anchor $\mathbf{0.4853}$ $\mathbf{8657}$ $\mathbf{115}$ \end{tabular}
elements.subsection-2411-012: The Gradient of the SDPO Objective
elements.sent-2411-196: As outlined in \cref{eq:obj5 , \cref{eq:obj6 , and \cref{eq:update , the SDPO objective incorporates several components with specific forms of stepwise weighting and clipping.}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: In the following, we derive the gradients for each of these equations.
elements.sent-2411-198: Firstly, the unclipped objective, presented in \cref{eq:obj5 , is given by: \begin{equation}l_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \left[ \left( \Delta \rho_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right)^2 \right],\end{equation} where $\Delta \rho_t(\theta) := \rho_t^a(\theta) - \rho_t^b(\theta)$ is the difference in log-likelihood ratios, and $\Delta \hat{A _t := \hat{A _t^a - \hat{A _t^b$ is the difference in advantage estimates for trajectories $a$ and $b$ at step $t$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}l_\theta(x_t^a, x_t^b, c) = E_{x_t^a, x_t^b, c} \left[ \left( \Delta \rho_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right)^2 \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-199: The gradient of $l_\theta$ w.r.t.. $\theta$ is: \begin{equation}\nabla_\theta l_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \left[ 2 \left( \Delta \rho_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} \cdot \nabla_\theta \Delta \rho_t(\theta) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\nabla_\theta l_\theta(x_t^a, x_t^b, c) = E_{x_t^a, x_t^b, c} \left[ 2 \left( \Delta \rho_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} \cdot \nabla_\theta \Delta \rho_t(\theta) \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-202: To stabilize updates, we constrain the log-likelihood ratios within a trust region by clipping them, yielding the clipped objective in \cref{eq:obj6 : \begin{equation}\tilde{l}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \left[ \left( \Delta \tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right)^2 \right],\end{equation} where $\Delta \tilde{\rho _t(\theta) := \tilde{\rho _t^a(\theta) - \tilde{\rho _t^b(\theta)$ and $\tilde{\rho ^j_t(\theta) := \text{clip (\rho^j_t(\theta), -\epsilon, \epsilon)$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\tilde{l}_\theta(x_t^a, x_t^b, c) = E_{x_t^a, x_t^b, c} \left[ \left( \Delta \tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right)^2 \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-203: The gradient for the clipped objective $\tilde{l _\theta$ w.r.t.. $\theta$ is: \begin{equation}\nabla_\theta \tilde{l}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_t^a, \mathbf{x}_t^b, \mathbf{c}} \left[ 2 \left( \Delta \tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} \cdot \nabla_\theta \Delta \tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \right].\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}\nabla_\theta \tilde{l}_\theta(x_t^a, x_t^b, c) = E_{x_t^a, x_t^b, c} \left[ 2 \left( \Delta \tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} - \Delta \hat{A}_t \right) \cdot \frac{\lambda^{(T - t - 1)}}{\eta} \cdot \nabla_\theta \Delta \tilde{\rho}_t(\theta) \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-205: Thus, for each term where $\rho_t^j(\theta)$ is clipped, the gradient will be zero: \begin{equation}\nabla_\theta \tilde{\rho}_t^j(\theta) = \begin{cases} \nabla_\theta \rho_t^j(\theta), \text{if } -\epsilon < \rho_t^j(\theta) < \epsilon, 0, \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}\end{equation} The final SDPO update, as defined in \cref{eq:update , employs a step-shuffled gradient update strategy and selects the maximum value between the unclipped and clipped objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-206: This stepwise update is formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_t(\theta) \leftarrow \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \max \left( l_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{a,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{x}^{b,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{c}_i), \tilde{l}_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{a,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{x}^{b,i}_{\tau_t^i}, \mathbf{c}_i) \right),\end{equation} where $\tau_i$ represents the shuffled step indices for the $i$-th batch.
elements.sent-2411-207: For each gradient update, we use the objective value corresponding to the maximum between $l_\theta$ and $\tilde{l _\theta$ for each sample in the batch.}
elements.sent-2411-209: This final gradient facilitates stable and efficient updates across steps by incorporating both the unclipped and clipped objectives in each stepwise update, selecting the one that better preserves stability at each step.
elements.section-2411-011: Additional Evaluation Curves for Step Generalization
elements.sent-2411-210: During the experiments in \cref{sec:4.2 exploring the step generalization of our model, we evaluated reward scores for images generated with different numbers of denoising steps beyond the 50-step denoised samples used in training.}
elements.sent-2411-211: While \cref{fig:1s shows evaluation curves for 1-step denoising, additional curves for 2-step, 4-step, 8-step, and 16-step denoising are presented in \cref{fig:2s,fig:4s,fig:8s,fig:16s , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Across all these evaluations, our SDPO consistently demonstrates superior step generalization performance compared to other algorithms.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/aes_2s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/img_2s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/hps_2s.pdf} \caption{Evaluation curves for generalization to 2-step. Reward scores (left: Aesthetic Score, middle: ImageReward, right: HPSv2) are evaluated for images generated with 2 denoising steps. The horizontal axis represents the cumulative number of training samples.} \label{fig:2s}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/aes_4s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/img_4s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/hps_4s.pdf} \caption{Evaluation curves for generalization to 4-step. Reward scores (left: Aesthetic Score, middle: ImageReward, right: HPSv2) are evaluated for images generated with 4 denoising steps. The horizontal axis represents the cumulative number of training samples.} \label{fig:4s}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/aes_8s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/img_8s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/hps_8s.pdf} \caption{Evaluation curves for generalization to 8-step. Reward scores (left: Aesthetic Score, middle: ImageReward, right: HPSv2) are evaluated for images generated with 8 denoising steps. The horizontal axis represents the cumulative number of training samples.} \label{fig:8s}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/aes_16s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/img_16s.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.33\textwidth]{figures/hps_16s.pdf} \caption{Evaluation curves for generalization to 16-step. Reward scores (left: Aesthetic Score, middle: ImageReward, right: HPSv2) are evaluated for images generated with 16 denoising steps. The horizontal axis represents the cumulative number of training samples.} \label{fig:16s}
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{figures/rlcm.pdf} \caption{Learning curves for latent consistency model. Our SDPO still outperforms DDPO and REBEL in reward optimization for the latent consistency model.} \label{fig:rlcm}
elements.sent-2411-213: In \cref{fig:vis1,fig:vis2,fig:vis3,fig:vis4,fig:vis5,fig:vis6 , we present additional samples from our SDPO, comparing with samples from DDPO, REBEL, D3PO, and pretrained SD-Turbo.}
elements.sent-2411-214: While low-step samples from the other methods exhibit lower visual quality and weaker alignment with the target reward structure, our SDPO consistently delivers high-quality, reward-aligned images across all sampling steps.

All images are generated with the same random seed.} \label{fig:vis1}
